claims.claim_text: The module names exact CKA-tested Service subskills that are more specific than the published exam domain description.
claims.anchor_text: You will be rigorously tested on your ability to rapidly expose deployments, correctly map target ports, debug complex connectivity failures, and differentiate the exact traffic routing mechanisms of ClusterIP, NodePort, and LoadBalancer configurations.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The CKA includes a Services and Networking domain, so you should expect hands-on Service creation, exposure, and troubleshooting tasks.
claims.rationale: The CKA page supports the domain and exam format, but not this exact list of subskills.

claims.claim_text: The learning outcome says `PreferSameNode` and `PreferSameZone` were introduced in Kubernetes v1.35.
claims.span_hint: What You'll Be Able to Do
claims.anchor_text: **Evaluate** advanced traffic distribution policies, directly contrasting `PreferSameNode` and `PreferSameZone` behaviors introduced in Kubernetes v1.35.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Evaluate** advanced traffic distribution policies, directly contrasting `PreferSameNode` and `PreferSameZone` behaviors available in Kubernetes v1.35.
claims.rationale: These values existed before v1.35; v1.35 is the stable point for the clearer naming, not the original introduction.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes Services provide a stable, long-lived network identity for backends even though the Pods behind them can change over time.
claims.rationale: The networking overview explicitly describes Services as stable IPs or hostnames for backends whose Pods can change over time.

claims.claim_text: The Service ClusterIP allocator divides the range using the formula `min(max(16, cidrSize / 16), 256)`.
claims.rationale: The virtual IP reference gives this exact formula for Service IP allocation bands.

claims.claim_text: EndpointSlices are stable as of Kubernetes v1.21, while the legacy Endpoints API is deprecated as of v1.33 and lacks support for newer Service features.
claims.rationale: The Service docs mark EndpointSlices stable in v1.21 and Endpoints deprecated in v1.33, and explain the newer-feature gap.

claims.claim_text: Service ClusterIP addresses are virtual IPs implemented by proxying logic rather than addresses answered by a single host interface.
claims.rationale: The virtual IP reference states that Service IPs are not answered by a single host and are implemented by packet-processing logic.

claims.claim_text: If `targetPort` is omitted for a Service port, Kubernetes defaults it to the same value as `port`.
claims.rationale: The Service docs explicitly document this defaulting behavior.

claims.claim_text: By default, every node runs kube-proxy unless an alternative implementation is deployed, and kube-proxy supports `iptables`, `ipvs`, and `nftables` on Linux and `kernelspace` on Windows.
claims.rationale: The virtual IP reference describes kube-proxy placement and the supported proxy modes by platform.

claims.claim_text: If kube-proxy proxy mode is unspecified, the default is `iptables` on Linux and `kernelspace` on Windows.
claims.rationale: The kube-proxy CLI reference directly names the default proxy mode on each OS.

claims.claim_text: If `spec.type` is omitted, a Service defaults to `ClusterIP`, which is reachable only from within the cluster.
claims.rationale: The Service docs state that `ClusterIP` is the default type and that it is cluster-internal only.

claims.claim_text: A headless Service (`clusterIP: None`) gets no ClusterIP, kube-proxy does not load-balance it, and DNS returns the backend Pod A/AAAA records directly.
claims.rationale: The headless Service section describes all three behaviors on the same page.

claims.claim_text: The module presents `LoadBalancer` as always routing through automatically created NodePorts.
claims.anchor_text: The `LoadBalancer` type natively integrates with underlying cloud providers (AWS, GCP, Azure) to provision an external, robust load balancer that automatically routes internet traffic into your cluster's NodePorts.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The `LoadBalancer` type asks the environment to provision an external load balancer for the Service; by default this usually builds on NodePort allocation, though some implementations can route directly to Pods.
claims.rationale: Kubernetes documents an exception where LoadBalancer Services can disable NodePort allocation.

claims.claim_text: For multi-port `LoadBalancer` Services, mixed protocols require the `MixedProtocolLBService` feature, which is stable in Kubernetes v1.26.
claims.rationale: The Service docs state the default same-protocol restriction and the stable feature gate version.

claims.claim_text: `ExternalName` Services return a DNS CNAME and do no proxying, and an `externalName` that looks like an IPv4 address is treated as a DNS name rather than an IP address.
claims.rationale: The ExternalName section directly covers both behaviors.

claims.claim_text: Service DNS names follow `<service>.<namespace>.svc.<cluster-domain>`, and Pod DNS search paths include the Pod namespace and cluster domain so short names resolve within a namespace.
claims.rationale: The DNS docs describe the naming pattern and default search-path behavior.

claims.claim_text: Service environment variables are only injected into Pods for Services that already existed before the Pod was created.
claims.rationale: The Service docs explicitly call out this ordering requirement for environment-variable discovery.

claims.claim_text: `internalTrafficPolicy` defaults to `Cluster`; `Local` routes only to node-local endpoints and drops traffic if there are no local endpoints.
claims.rationale: The Service API reference directly documents the default and Local-mode behavior.

claims.claim_text: In Kubernetes v1.35, `trafficDistribution` supports `PreferSameZone` and `PreferSameNode`, and `PreferClose` is a deprecated alias for `PreferSameZone`.
claims.rationale: The current Service docs list these v1.35 values and the deprecated alias relationship.

claims.claim_text: The note about official documentation being internally inconsistent is a transient editorial observation and should be rewritten to the current stable API names.
claims.anchor_text: *Architectural Note*: The canonical set of `trafficDistribution` string values is currently internally inconsistent across official documentation and raw API references. While API references occasionally showcase `PreferClose` as a legacy alias, definitive concept documentation and the official v1.35 release notes designate `PreferSameZone` and `PreferSameNode` as the correct, modernized nomenclature.
claims.suggested_rewrite: *Architectural Note*: In Kubernetes v1.35, use `PreferSameZone` and `PreferSameNode`; `PreferClose` remains as a deprecated alias for `PreferSameZone`.
claims.rationale: The teaching point is the current API vocabulary, not a time-sensitive commentary about documentation drift.

claims.claim_text: A Service without a selector does not get automatic endpoint discovery, and can instead front manually defined Endpoints or EndpointSlices for external backends.
claims.rationale: The Service docs explain selectorless Services and their manual-backend use cases.

claims.claim_text: The API server refuses `kubectl port-forward` against a Service without a selector because the endpoints are not mapped to Pods.
claims.rationale: The Service docs explicitly call out this port-forward restriction.

claims.claim_text: `externalIPs` are user-managed addresses; Kubernetes does not allocate or manage them.
claims.rationale: The Service API reference states that `externalIPs` are not managed by Kubernetes.

claims.claim_text: The module ties nftables tracing specifically to Kubernetes v1.35+, but the operational point is really about clusters using kube-proxy in nftables mode.
claims.anchor_text: For modern clusters executing Kubernetes v1.35 or higher and running the high-performance `nftables` mode, you would instead execute `nft list ruleset | grep 10.96.45.123` to trace corresponding Network Address Translation (NAT) table structures.
claims.suggested_rewrite: On clusters running kube-proxy in `nftables` mode, you would instead execute `nft list ruleset | grep 10.96.45.123` to trace the corresponding NAT rules.
claims.rationale: The version qualifier is unnecessary and over-specific for the documented behavior.

claims.claim_text: Service selectors use equality-based label matching, and matching Pods may have additional labels beyond those in the selector.
claims.rationale: The labels documentation describes equality-based matching and explicitly allows additional labels on matching objects.

claims.claim_text: The knowledge-check answer presents LoadBalancer as inherently costing money per load balancer, which is a spend claim better rewritten without an uncited pricing assertion.
claims.anchor_text: (2) Change to LoadBalancer -- clean external IP, but costs money per LB in cloud environments;
claims.suggested_rewrite: (2) Change to LoadBalancer -- gives you a cloud-managed external endpoint, but depends on cloud-provider support and operational trade-offs;
claims.rationale: This is a pricing claim without a cited, current provider source and is not needed for the teaching point.

claims.claim_text: The knowledge-check answer states as fact that `kubectl expose` cannot set a specific `nodePort`; the safe teaching point is the YAML workflow.
claims.anchor_text: The imperative approach requires YAML since `kubectl expose` cannot set a specific nodePort.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A practical approach is to generate YAML, add `nodePort: 30100`, and apply it.
claims.rationale: The docs clearly support the YAML workflow, while the negative capability claim is less directly documented.

further_reading.title: Service
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary upstream reference for Service types, headless Services, ExternalName, selectorless Services, and EndpointSlice deprecation context.

further_reading.title: Virtual IPs and Service Proxies
further_reading.why_relevant: Best reference for kube-proxy modes, virtual IP behavior, traffic policies, session affinity, and Service IP allocation.

further_reading.title: DNS for Services and Pods
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers Service DNS names, search paths, cross-namespace lookup behavior, and headless-Service DNS records.

notes: Excluded analogies, quizzes-as-questions, diagrams, tables, and code blocks per instructions. Repeated factual claims were consolidated unless a later occurrence introduced a new unsupported or over-specific assertion that needs its own rewrite.
